label: PFAS Threatens Great Lakes Fish & Water

comments.text: With the roll back of clean drinking water rules around PFAS, it’s time to start banging pots and pans about the impact in Michigan. I teamed up with Great Lakes PFAS Action for a town hall in Oscoda to hear directly from folks living with the impact of PFAS contamination.

Michigan was one of the first to issue PFAS warnings about the foam and the dangers of swimming or eating the fish. It's why I teamed up with Great Lakes PFAS Action for a town hall in Oscoda. This isn’t partisan, it’s about clean water.

Commentary: A review of plastic "forever chemical" studies from each of the Great Lakes found the highest PFAS concentrations in Lake Ontario and Lake Erie, which represents the vast majority of Ohio's northern border ohiocapitaljournal.com/2026/02/23/p...

New PFAS analytical method from our Great Lakes contaminants lab. We had issues reliably extracting PFAS from whole fish samples using Method 1633, so we found that lipid-extraction resulted in clean extracts. The work was led by my awesome, but Bluesky-less colleagues. doi.org/10.1021/acs....

The Great Lakes PFAS Action Network has launched a “Eat Safe Fish” mapping tool, which collects “Do Not Eat” and consumption advisories into an interactive map, allowing anglers to examine where contamination has been detected and which fish are safe to eat. From .bsky.social

Discourse continues regarding the effects of #PFAS in our water and other media, but how do PFAS get there in the first place? A new article in The Conservation breaks down how PFAS are introduced into the #GreatLakes.

Amid PFAS fears, oversimplified warnings could discourage residents from consuming a food central to Ojibwe lifeways.

ICYMI: Farewell PFAS. A GLPF-supported project is testing technology that goes beyond removing PFAS from water. It destroys them completely. This is real progress, turning breakthrough science into lasting clean water for the Great Lakes region.

PFAS are among the toughest pollutants to eliminate. A GLPF-backed project is testing a breakthrough method that destroys them outright. This is a leap toward clean water for all who depend on the Great Lakes.

PFAS are some of the most persistent pollutants in our waters, but they’re not invincible. A Michigan State University led team is using plasma technology to capture and permanently destroy PFAS. The result: scalable tools to safeguard the Great Lakes.

PFAS levels in Great Lakes fish are dropping, according to a recent report from EPA researchers.

Chief of Division of Surface Water Holly Tucker notes Ohio is the only Great Lakes state that doesn’t have a PFAS advisory for fish consumption. She says they are testing fish in Lake Erie for PFAS and that they are not finding levels that indicate a need for limits.

"The wastewater contained the following pollutants, per the report: E. coli bacteria
Oil
Grease
Suspended solids
Other unnamed pollutants The lawsuit claimed that the discharges were fueling toxic algae blooms in Lake Erie, which can threaten drinking water supplies and harm wildlife."

.. Sport and pleasure fishing took a big hit here,nearly 40 years ago..when we discovered the fish we caught and ate out of Lake Erie were contaminated.. High levels or mercury..among other things.. like cancerous growths inside the fish.. Commercial fishing came to a dead stop..
